notable.notable_blurb: Hartford-born mother of President Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: A Georgia belle who inspired Scarlett O'Hara
narrative_blocks.body: Mittie was a celebrated Southern beauty raised at Bulloch Hall in Roswell, Georgia, said to have partly inspired Margaret Mitchell's Scarlett O'Hara. Through the Civil War she remained an unreconstructed Confederate while her husband worked for the Union.

narrative_blocks.header: Two deaths in one house on Valentine's Day
narrative_blocks.body: On Saint Valentine's Day in 1884 Mittie died of typhoid in the family home as her son Theodore kept vigil. One floor above, his first wife was dying the same day, hours after bearing his daughter.

bio.bio_blurb: Hartford-born mother of President Theodore Roosevelt

notable.notable_blurb: Merchant-philanthropist; father of President Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: He helped found three great New York institutions
narrative_blocks.body: A glass-importing merchant and tireless philanthropist, Roosevelt helped establish the Metropolitan Museum of Art, the American Museum of Natural History, and the Children's Aid Society. He poured himself into charity for the city's poor children.

narrative_blocks.header: He sold Lincoln on a soldiers' pay plan
narrative_blocks.body: Devoted to the Union though his wife's sympathies ran Confederate, Roosevelt devised an allotment system letting soldiers send pay home to their families. He lobbied it through Washington with Lincoln's help and toured the Army of the Potomac signing men up.

cross_connections.display_label: the Hooker descendant his son President Roosevelt married

bio.bio_blurb: Merchant-philanthropist; father of President Theodore Roosevelt

notable.notable_blurb: 26th US President; Rough Rider; Nobel Peace laureate

cross_connections.display_label: his Harvard ornithology partner on an 1877 bird list
Trust-busted Morgan's Northern Securities Company in 1904
designed the townhouse his family used near the White House
drew up the guardianship papers for his children
the Columbia president and Nobel laureate he befriended

military_service.notes: Led the assault on the San Juan Heights, 1898; Medal of Honor awarded in 2001.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the childhood friend next door
narrative_blocks.body: Roosevelt married Edith Kermit Carow in London in 1886, his second wife and a granddaughter of the Union general Daniel Tyler. Through her he entered the Tyler family, with its Edwards and Hooker roots in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard ornithology friendship became his first publication
narrative_blocks.body: He met Henry Davis Minot, a Boston-based naturalist, as a Harvard freshman in 1877. They spent vacations listing birds in the Adirondacks and co-authored 'The Summer Birds of the Adirondacks' that fall — his first published work.

narrative_blocks.header: Trust-busted J.P. Morgan's Northern Securities Company
narrative_blocks.body: His administration brought the antitrust suit that dissolved Morgan's Northern Securities holding company in 1904, ending the merger of the Northern Pacific and Great Northern railroads. Morgan reportedly told him 'Send your man to my man and they can fix it up' — the President refused, and the Supreme Court sided with the government.

narrative_blocks.header: He befriended Columbia's president Nicholas Butler
narrative_blocks.body: Roosevelt's circle included Nicholas Murray Butler, the long-serving Columbia president and 1931 Nobel peace laureate, whom he nicknamed Nicholas Miraculous. The two Republican allies stayed close from Roosevelt's rise through the White House years.

narrative_blocks.header: The Roosevelts borrowed a famous architect's townhouse
narrative_blocks.body: When the White House was torn up for renovation in 1902, the family decamped to a townhouse belonging to the firm of Carrère and Hastings. Its partner Thomas Hastings would go on to design the New York Public Library and Arlington's Tomb of the Unknowns.

narrative_blocks.header: She died two days after giving birth
narrative_blocks.body: Roosevelt's first wife died of Bright's disease on February 14, 1884, two days after their daughter Alice was born and hours after his own mother died in the same house. Theodore marked his diary with a black cross.

notable.notable_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: She built the modern First Lady's office
narrative_blocks.body: Edith hired the first salaried social secretary for a First Lady and directed the 1902 White House renovation that created the West Wing. Historians credit her with shaping the modern institution of the role.

narrative_blocks.header: She burned a lifetime of love letters
narrative_blocks.body: After Theodore died, the intensely private Edith destroyed the letters he had written her across their courtship and marriage. It is part of why her inner life stayed, by her own design, almost unknowable.

narrative_blocks.header: She wrote her own Connecticut ancestors' history
narrative_blocks.body: Late in life Edith researched her mother's Tyler family and co-wrote a history of their colonial Connecticut roots. She bought the family's ancestral home in Brooklyn, Connecticut, returning there each summer.

narrative_blocks.header: She stumped for Hoover to disown Franklin
narrative_blocks.body: Edith kept out of politics for decades, then made a rare appearance for Herbert Hoover in 1932. Part of her motive was to remind voters that the Democratic nominee, Franklin Roosevelt, was only a distant cousin and not her son.

narrative_blocks.header: Theodore regretted overruling her judgment
narrative_blocks.body: A childhood neighbor whom Theodore Roosevelt married in 1886, Edith ran Sagamore Hill and raised six children while quietly advising him on people and politics. Roosevelt admitted he was worse off whenever he ignored her counsel.

cross_connections.display_label: was her stepdaughter, the capital’s sharpest wit
her husband President Roosevelt's Hartford-born mother

bio.bio_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

career.role: First Lady of the United States

narrative_blocks.header: Washington's sharpest tongue for seventy years
narrative_blocks.body: Longworth reigned over Washington society for decades from her Massachusetts Avenue salon, feared for her wit and her feuds with both Roosevelt cousins. A pillow on her sofa read, 'If you can't say something good, sit here by me.'

narrative_blocks.header: If you can't say anything nice, sit here
narrative_blocks.body: She kept a sofa cushion embroidered with that line and lived by it, skewering presidents for six decades. She dismissed Thomas Dewey as the little man on the wedding cake and would not call her cousin anything but Franklin.

narrative_blocks.header: Pet snake, cigarettes, the White House roof
narrative_blocks.body: The president's irreverent eldest daughter smoked on the White House roof when forbidden to below and carried a pet snake to receptions. Her father said he could run the country or control Alice, but not both.

narrative_blocks.header: Princess Alice toured Asia for her father
narrative_blocks.body: At twenty-one she joined the 1905 Taft mission to Japan, China, and the Philippines as the president's envoy-celebrity, mobbed everywhere as Princess Alice. Her favorite blue-gray set off a national craze called Alice blue, even a hit song.

narrative_blocks.header: The 1906 White House wedding of the year
narrative_blocks.body: In February 1906 she married Ohio congressman Nicholas Longworth in the East Room before all of official Washington, the most watched wedding of its day. The match cooled after he backed Taft over her father, and she outlived him by nearly fifty years.

cross_connections.display_label: was her stepmother in the White House

notable.notable_blurb: Medal of Honor general; eldest Roosevelt son

narrative_blocks.header: Only general in D-Day's first wave
narrative_blocks.body: At fifty-six, with a heart condition and walking with a cane, Roosevelt was the only general to land with the first wave at Utah Beach on June 6, 1944. He died of a heart attack five weeks later and received the Medal of Honor posthumously.

narrative_blocks.header: He turned a landing error into a foothold
narrative_blocks.body: When the first craft drifted a mile south of their mark, he walked the fire-swept beach, met each arriving regiment, and improvised the attack from where they actually stood. Bradley later called it the bravest thing he saw in the war.

narrative_blocks.header: A father-son Medal of Honor, a century apart
narrative_blocks.body: His posthumous Medal of Honor and his father's, awarded at last in 2001 for San Juan Hill, made the Roosevelts only the second father and son both to earn it. The other pair were the two MacArthurs.

narrative_blocks.header: Father and son both hit the beaches
narrative_blocks.body: He was the only father to land with a son on D-Day; his boy Quentin came ashore at Omaha while he led at Utah. The family's soldiering instinct had carried into a second world war.

narrative_blocks.header: Dead of a heart attack five weeks later
narrative_blocks.body: He never saw the medal coming; a heart attack killed him in Normandy about five weeks after the landing. He was buried near the front and later moved to lie beside his brother Quentin, lost flying in the first war.

bio.bio_blurb: Medal of Honor general; eldest Roosevelt son

military_service.awards: Distinguished Service Cross; French Croix de Guerre
military_service.notes: Wounded at Soissons and gassed at Cantigny.

military_service.awards: Medal of Honor (posthumous, presented to his widow September 1944)
military_service.notes: Operation Torch (North Africa), Tunisia, and Sicily before leading the first wave ashore at Utah Beach on D-Day; died of a heart attack at Meautis, France, July 12, 1944.

notable.notable_blurb: Explorer and author; son of Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: He died by his own hand in Alaska
narrative_blocks.body: Kermit joined his father on the African safari and the near-fatal Amazon expedition, and fought in both World Wars, first in the British army. Long burdened by alcoholism and depression, he died by suicide at an army post in Alaska in 1943.

narrative_blocks.header: He helped found a transatlantic steamship line
narrative_blocks.body: An explorer and writer, Roosevelt co-founded the Roosevelt Steamship Company, which became part of the United States Lines. He published books on hunting and travel.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept his father alive on the Amazon
narrative_blocks.body: On the 1914 expedition down Brazil's River of Doubt, Kermit rationed his own quinine to his malarial father and was credited with saving the former President's life. He nearly died of the fever himself.

narrative_blocks.header: He won the Military Cross in Mesopotamia
narrative_blocks.body: In 1917 he took an honorary captaincy in the British Army and fought under General Maude in the Mesopotamian campaign, where he earned the Military Cross. He crossed to the American artillery in France for the war's last offensive.

narrative_blocks.header: He tore through Harvard in record time
narrative_blocks.body: A voracious reader, he compressed the four-year Harvard course into about two and a half years, breaking off after his freshman year to join his father's African safari. He took his degree in 1912.

cross_connections.display_label: re-ran the River of Doubt eight decades later

bio.bio_blurb: Explorer, soldier, son of Theodore Roosevelt

military_service.unit: 14th Light Armored Motor Battery, Machine Gun Corps
military_service.notes: Served under General Maude in the Mesopotamian campaign, 1917-1918.
Transferred to the American Expeditionary Force in France, 1918; Meuse-Argonne Offensive.
Intelligence officer; helped raise a territorial militia of Alaska Natives.

narrative_blocks.header: She nursed the wounded in wartime France
narrative_blocks.body: Early in the First World War she went to Paris with her husband and worked at the American Ambulance Hospital while he operated on men brought in from the front. She came home to decades of civic and hospital work on Long Island.

narrative_blocks.header: She made Sagamore Hill a national shrine
narrative_blocks.body: More than anyone she pushed to preserve her father's home, Sagamore Hill, as a public historic site, and guarded the Roosevelt papers given to Harvard. In Oyster Bay, where she fought for low-income housing, neighbors called her the Queen of Oyster Bay.

narrative_blocks.header: One of the museum's first women trustees
narrative_blocks.body: She became one of the first two women on the board of the American Museum of Natural History, the institution her grandfather had helped found. She also chaired the Nassau County Nursing Service and led the county's Red Cross.

narrative_blocks.header: Painted in her Red Cross uniform
narrative_blocks.body: For her formal family portrait she chose to sit in her Red Cross uniform rather than evening dress and jewels. She gave the organization some sixty years and chaired its Nassau County work.

narrative_blocks.header: The Roosevelt child who stayed close to home
narrative_blocks.body: Of all the president's children she was the steady one, anchoring the family at Oyster Bay long after her brothers scattered to wars and far places. She married the surgeon who would tend the wounded beside her in France.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, a New York eye-surgery pioneer
was her mother-in-law, painted by John Singer Sargent

bio.bio_blurb: TR's only surviving daughter by Edith Carow

notable.notable_blurb: TR's son; wounded and decorated in two wars

narrative_blocks.header: The only man twice ruined by one wound
narrative_blocks.body: A grenade in New Guinea in 1943 shattered the same knee German fire had wrecked in France in 1918, leaving him the only American ever rated totally disabled for one wound in two wars. He came home with four Silver Stars.

narrative_blocks.header: The old lion is dead
narrative_blocks.body: When Theodore Roosevelt died in January 1919, Archibald wired the news to his scattered brothers and sister in five blunt words. He was still recovering from his French wounds at the time.

narrative_blocks.header: From Teapot Dome to a bond house
narrative_blocks.body: He resigned from Sinclair Oil during the Teapot Dome inquiry and, though never implicated, gave testimony damaging to his former employer. After the second war he founded the municipal-bond firm Roosevelt and Cross, which still trades today.

narrative_blocks.header: A hard turn to the far right
narrative_blocks.body: In his later years he joined the John Birch Society and founded the Veritas Foundation to root out what he called socialist influence at Harvard. In 1954 he published a pamphlet attacking the award of a Theodore Roosevelt medal to the diplomat Ralph Bunche.

narrative_blocks.header: The last of Edith's sons
narrative_blocks.body: He outlived every brother, the only son of Theodore and Edith to come through both World Wars alive. His stone at Oyster Bay reads, the old fighting man home from the wars.

bio.bio_blurb: TR's son; wounded and decorated in two wars

military_service.notes: Severely wounded in France and discharged with full disability; two Silver Star citations and the Croix de Guerre.
Commanded in New Guinea; wounded at the ridge named in his honor.

notable.notable_blurb: Roosevelt's aviator son, killed in action 1918

narrative_blocks.header: Shot down behind enemy lines at twenty
narrative_blocks.body: On July 14, 1918, his patrol met German fighters over Chamery and his plane went down behind enemy lines. German troops buried the president's son with full military honors, and the grave became a shrine that thousands of American soldiers came to see.

narrative_blocks.header: The White House's most incorrigible small boy
narrative_blocks.body: He shot spitballs at the official presidential portraits, flung snowballs from the roof at passersby, and once gouged a baseball diamond into the South Lawn. His father, who quietly counted him the favorite, mostly delighted in it.

narrative_blocks.header: He left Harvard for the cockpit
narrative_blocks.body: Admitted to Harvard like his father and brothers, he dropped out in 1917 at his father's urging to train as a pursuit pilot. He shipped to France and eventually flew with the Ninety-fifth Aero Squadron.

narrative_blocks.header: Reinterred beside the brother he idolized
narrative_blocks.body: In 1955 his remains were moved to the Normandy American Cemetery to lie beside Ted, who had died after leading men ashore on D-Day. A Long Island airfield, and much later a shopping center, carried his name.

narrative_blocks.header: His death benefits went to a small nephew
narrative_blocks.body: He left what he had to his sister Ethel's young son, a boy who had taken to pointing at passing planes and calling them Uncle Quentin. The child would die only four years after Quentin himself.

cross_connections.display_label: a private killed near Saint-Mihiel that same 1918
his namesake nephew, also lost in an air crash

bio.bio_blurb: TR's youngest son; WWI pilot killed in France

military_service.awards: French Croix de Guerre with Palm
military_service.notes: Pursuit pilot shot down and killed over Chamery, France, July 14, 1918.

burialCemetery.name: Green-Wood Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Historic Brooklyn cemetery. Burial place of Dr. Charles Lancelot Minor (HD1607) and Mary McDowell Venable Minor (X00990).
